class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Biostatistics and Study Design
learning_objectives: Explain why biostatistics is essential for health informatics
Differentiate between descriptive and inferential statistics
Identify common study designs in health research
readings: Biostatistics for the Health Sciences Ch. 1-2
Guyatt G. Users' Guide to the Medical Literature Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Biostatistics provides the mathematical foundation for evidence-based healthcare. Health informatics professionals must understand statistical concepts to design systems that correctly calculate quality measures, evaluate interventions, and support clinical research.
key_concepts: Biostatistics role in health informatics: quality measures, analytics, research, CDS
Descriptive statistics: summarizing health data (mean, median, mode, SD, range, IQR)
Inferential statistics: drawing conclusions about populations from samples
Study designs: RCT, cohort, case-control, cross-sectional, ecological
Variables: independent, dependent, confounding, continuous, categorical
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculate descriptive statistics for a dataset of patient blood pressure readings using Python, interpreting the clinical significance of each measure

review.value: Descriptive statistics summarize what we see in the data; inferential statistics use the data to draw conclusions about a larger population
A variable associated with both the exposure and outcome that distorts the apparent relationship - must be controlled for in analysis

learning_objectives: Apply probability concepts to clinical scenarios
Understand the normal distribution and its role in health data
Interpret diagnostic test performance using Bayes' theorem
readings: Biostatistics for the Health Sciences Ch. 3-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Probability underpins all statistical inference. In healthcare, probability concepts drive diagnostic test interpretation, risk assessment, and clinical decision-making.
key_concepts: Basic probability: events, sample space, conditional probability, independence
Normal (Gaussian) distribution: properties, z-scores, standard normal table
Central Limit Theorem: why sample means are normally distributed
Diagnostic test metrics: sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV
Bayes' theorem: how disease prevalence affects test predictive values
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculate the PPV of a screening test at different disease prevalence rates, demonstrating why population-level screening has different implications than targeted testing

review.value: True Positive Rate: proportion of actual positives correctly identified by the test - TP/(TP+FN)
PPV depends on prevalence: even a highly specific test produces many false positives when disease is rare

learning_objectives: Formulate null and alternative hypotheses for clinical questions
Interpret p-values and confidence intervals correctly
Understand Type I and Type II errors in clinical research
readings: Biostatistics for the Health Sciences Ch. 6-7

lecture_notes.introduction: Hypothesis testing is the framework for determining whether observed differences are likely real or due to chance. Correct interpretation of p-values and confidence intervals is critical for evidence-based practice.
key_concepts: Null hypothesis (H0) and alternative hypothesis (H1)
P-value: probability of observing the data (or more extreme) if H0 is true
Significance level (alpha): conventional threshold of 0.05
Confidence intervals: range of plausible values for the population parameter
Type I error (false positive, alpha) and Type II error (false negative, beta)
Power: probability of detecting a true effect (1 - beta)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Test whether a new medication lowers blood pressure compared to placebo using a two-sample t-test, interpreting the p-value and 95% CI

review.value: P-value is NOT the probability that the null hypothesis is true. It is the probability of the observed data given that the null hypothesis IS true.
95% CI: if we repeated the study many times, 95% of calculated intervals would contain the true population parameter - provides both estimate and uncertainty

class_schedule.topic: Comparing Groups: t-tests, ANOVA, and Chi-square
learning_objectives: Select the appropriate statistical test for different data types and comparisons
Perform t-tests, ANOVA, and chi-square tests on clinical data
Interpret test results in clinical context
readings: Biostatistics for the Health Sciences Ch. 8-10

lecture_notes.introduction: Different clinical questions require different statistical tests. Choosing the right test depends on the data type, number of groups, and research design.
key_concepts: One-sample t-test: comparing a sample mean to a known value
Two-sample t-test: comparing means between two groups (treatment vs. control)
Paired t-test: comparing before/after measurements in the same subjects
One-way ANOVA: comparing means across 3+ groups
Chi-square test: comparing proportions between categorical groups
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare 30-day readmission rates across 3 hospital units using chi-square, and compare length of stay between two surgical approaches using a two-sample t-test

review.value: Continuous outcome, 2 groups: t-test. Continuous outcome, 3+ groups: ANOVA. Categorical outcome, 2+ groups: chi-square
Paired: same subjects measured twice (before/after). Independent: different subjects in each group (treatment/control)

learning_objectives: Build linear regression models for continuous health outcomes
Build logistic regression models for binary health outcomes
Interpret regression coefficients in clinical context
readings: Biostatistics for the Health Sciences Ch. 12-13

lecture_notes.introduction: Regression allows us to model relationships between variables and control for confounders. It is the workhorse of clinical research and healthcare analytics.
key_concepts: Simple linear regression: one predictor, continuous outcome (e.g., age predicting BP)
Multiple linear regression: multiple predictors, controlling for confounders
Logistic regression: binary outcome (readmitted yes/no), odds ratios
Model interpretation: coefficients, p-values, confidence intervals, R-squared
Assumptions: linearity, independence, normality of residuals, homoscedasticity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a logistic regression model predicting 30-day readmission using age, LOS, comorbidity count, and insurance type; interpret the odds ratios

review.value: In logistic regression, exp(coefficient) gives the odds ratio: OR > 1 means the predictor increases odds of the outcome; OR < 1 means it decreases odds
Multiple regression estimates the effect of each predictor while holding others constant, isolating the independent contribution of each variable

learning_objectives: Understand survival analysis methods for time-to-event data
Interpret Kaplan-Meier curves and log-rank tests
Evaluate clinical trial results using statistical principles
readings: Biostatistics for the Health Sciences Ch. 14
Pocock SJ. Clinical Trials: A Practical Approach (selected sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Survival analysis handles time-to-event data with censoring, common in clinical trials and longitudinal health studies. Understanding these methods is essential for evaluating treatment effectiveness.
key_concepts: Time-to-event data: death, readmission, disease progression, time on ventilator
Censoring: when the event is not observed during follow-up (patient drops out, study ends)
Kaplan-Meier estimator: estimating survival probability over time
Log-rank test: comparing survival curves between groups
Cox proportional hazards model: regression for survival data (hazard ratios)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze survival data from a simulated clinical trial comparing two cancer treatments: generate Kaplan-Meier curves, perform log-rank test, and fit a Cox model

review.value: Step function estimating the probability of surviving beyond each time point, accounting for censoring - the standard method for visualizing survival data
From Cox regression: HR > 1 means higher hazard (worse outcome) in the treatment group; HR < 1 means lower hazard (better outcome) compared to reference

questions.question: A hospital implements a sepsis screening tool with 85% sensitivity and 75% specificity. If the prevalence of sepsis in the screened population is 8%, calculate the Positive Predictive Value. Explain what this means for the clinical team using this tool.
questions.answer: PPV = (Sensitivity x Prevalence) / [(Sensitivity x Prevalence) + ((1-Specificity) x (1-Prevalence))]. PPV = (0.85 x 0.08) / [(0.85 x 0.08) + (0.25 x 0.92)] = 0.068 / (0.068 + 0.23) = 0.068 / 0.298 = 22.8%. This means that when the screening tool flags a patient as positive for sepsis, there is only about a 23% chance the patient actually has sepsis. Roughly 3 out of 4 positive screens will be false positives. The clinical team needs to understand that a positive screen requires further workup rather than immediate treatment, and that alert fatigue is a serious concern with this tool's performance at this prevalence.

rubric.key: Correct PPV calculation with formula shown
Discussion of implications for clinical workflow

questions.question: A researcher reports that a new drug reduces blood pressure by 5 mmHg compared to placebo (p = 0.03, 95% CI: 1.2 to 8.8 mmHg). A colleague says 'there is a 97% probability that the drug works.' Explain why this interpretation is wrong and provide the correct interpretation of the p-value and confidence interval.
questions.answer: The colleague's interpretation is wrong. A p-value of 0.03 does NOT mean there is a 97% probability the drug works. The p-value means: if the null hypothesis were true (the drug has no effect), there is a 3% probability of observing a difference of 5 mmHg or greater by chance alone. It is a statement about the data given the null hypothesis, not a statement about the probability of the hypothesis. Correct interpretation of the CI: we are 95% confident that the true population mean difference in blood pressure lies between 1.2 and 8.8 mmHg. Since the CI does not include 0, this is consistent with rejecting the null hypothesis at alpha = 0.05. Both the p-value and CI suggest the drug has a statistically significant effect, but clinical significance must also be considered (is a 5 mmHg reduction clinically meaningful?).

rubric.key: Correctly identifies why the interpretation is wrong

questions.question: A quality improvement team wants to compare surgical site infection rates across 4 hospital units. They have categorical data (infected yes/no) for each unit. Which statistical test is appropriate and why? What would the null hypothesis be?
questions.answer: The appropriate test is the chi-square test of independence (or equivalence). It is appropriate because: (1) the outcome is categorical (infected vs. not infected), (2) there are more than 2 groups (4 hospital units), and (3) the observations are independent. The null hypothesis is: there is no association between hospital unit and surgical site infection rate (i.e., the infection rates are the same across all 4 units). If the chi-square test is significant (p < 0.05), post-hoc pairwise comparisons with Bonferroni correction would be needed to determine which specific units differ. Note: if any expected cell count is less than 5, Fisher's exact test should be used instead.

questions.question: A logistic regression model predicting 30-day readmission produces these results for the variable 'number of comorbidities': coefficient = 0.35, OR = 1.42, 95% CI for OR: (1.15, 1.75), p = 0.001. Interpret these results clinically.
questions.answer: Interpretation: For each additional comorbidity a patient has, the odds of 30-day readmission increase by 42% (OR = 1.42), holding all other variables in the model constant. The 95% confidence interval (1.15 to 1.75) tells us the true odds ratio likely falls between a 15% and 75% increase per additional comorbidity. Since the CI does not include 1.0 and the p-value (0.001) is well below 0.05, this is a statistically significant predictor. Clinical significance: this is a clinically meaningful finding suggesting that patients with higher comorbidity burden are substantially more likely to be readmitted. Care management resources should be preferentially allocated to high-comorbidity patients at discharge. The effect size (42% increase per comorbidity) is large enough to be actionable.

rubric.key: Correct interpretation of CI and p-value

major_assignments.title: Biostatistical Analysis of a Healthcare Dataset
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive biostatistical analysis of a healthcare dataset, including descriptive statistics, hypothesis testing, regression modeling, and clinical interpretation of results.
objectives: Apply biostatistical methods to a real-world healthcare dataset
Select and justify appropriate statistical tests for clinical questions
Interpret statistical results in clinical context
Communicate findings to a non-statistical audience
deliverables: Jupyter notebook with complete statistical analysis
At least 3 hypothesis tests with clinical question framing
Written summary (5 pages) interpreting results for a clinical audience

criteria: Appropriate summary statistics for each variable
Clinical significance discussed alongside statistical significance
Thorough descriptive analysis with clinical context
Correct statistical test selection and interpretation
Well-specified regression model with clinical utility
Clear communication of findings for non-statistical audience

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of biostatistical concepts and calculation skills

lab_assignments.description: Weekly computational statistics exercises using Python

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive biostatistical analysis of a healthcare dataset

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of probability, hypothesis testing, and basic tests

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including regression, survival analysis, and interpretation

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory. Statistics requires consistent practice. More than 2 unexcused lab absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. All code must produce correct output to receive credit.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of statistical concepts is encouraged. All analysis code and interpretation must be individually produced.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All statistical analyses must be the student's own work. Use of AI for code generation must be disclosed and the student must explain methodology choices.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Python (Anaconda) with scipy, statsmodels, scikit-learn, lifelines. Jupyter notebooks. Statistical reference tables or software.
